subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Consistently foregrounds the key concept of change throughout the report
Clear analytical links made between âchangeâ and Microsoftâs market share, brand image and financials
Demonstrates solid understanding of how the acquisition drove strategic change
weaknesses: Key concept is sometimes inserted superficially rather than critically explored
Lacks a clear theoretical definition or framework for âchangeâ at the outset
Occasional assertions (e.g., âradically changedâ) are unsupported by data or citation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Band 4: The concept of change is repeatedly and coherently linked to organisational analysis, showing strong understanding with partially integrated analysis. Occasional superficial use and lack of formal definition prevent top-band performance.

strengths: Provides three relevant supporting documents plus annual report extracts
Includes both qualitative (SWOT paper, Investopedia) and quantitative (financial statements) sources
weaknesses: In-text citations use placeholders and lack consistent formatting
Ambiguity in footnotes (e.g., repeated references to âSupporting Document 3â)
Missing direct source citations for ratio tables and inconsistent bibliography style
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reduced to band 2 due to citation and formatting inconsistencies. Although the quantity and relevance meet band 3, the uneven depth and referencing errors limit the band-3 descriptor for in-depth and well-documented sources.

strengths: Selects three classic tools (SWOT, Ansoff Matrix, ratio analysis) covering strategic and financial perspectives
Tools are clearly labelled and linked to sections of the report
weaknesses: SWOT entries remain generic without firm-specific metrics or competitor data
Analysis focuses almost solely on market penetration in Ansoff, neglecting other growth strategies
No use of more advanced frameworks (e.g., Porterâs Five Forces, VRIO) to deepen analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Placed in band 2: selection and application are present but only superficially relevant. The limited depth of application and absence of broader or industry-specific theories preclude band 3.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Uses pre- and post-deal financial figures to support discussion of profitability and liquidity
Links NPM changes to acquisition costs and strategic opportunities in cloud gaming
SWOT tables and ratio outputs are interpreted rather than merely presented
weaknesses: Several data sources are unclear or undocumented
Analytical commentary often restates tool outputs without critical evaluation
Lacks industry benchmarks or competitor comparisons to contextualise ratios
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Band 3: Adequate data selection and some analysis of the research question. The work goes beyond description but stops short of mostly effective or sustained integration needed for band 4.

strengths: Conclusion directly addresses the research question and aligns with evidence from SWOT and financial analysis
Provides a coherent summary that Microsoftâs competitiveness has improved post-acquisition
weaknesses: Does not consider counter-arguments or potential pitfalls of the acquisition
Fails to quantify âto what extentâ the question is answered or provide balanced judgement
Relies on general statements rather than rigorous evidence weighting
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Band 2: Presents conclusions that are partially consistent with evidence and offer a partial answer. The absence of counter-analysis and quantification prevents band 3.

strengths: Clear overall structure with introduction, tool sections, conclusion and bibliography
Logical table of contents with page references aids navigation
Headings are meaningful and information flows coherently
weaknesses: Sub-section numbering could better match contents entries
Conclusion is not clearly demarcated as a distinct section
Some repetition in opportunities narrative disrupts conciseness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Band 2: Appropriate structure is maintained throughout. Minor organisational tweaks would improve clarity but the report already meets the descriptor for effective structure.

strengths: Includes all required SL IA elements: title page, research question, key concept, word count, contents page
LaTeXârendered ratio formulas enhance professional presentation
weaknesses: Bibliography formatting is inconsistent (italics, inline symbols)
Citation placeholders and formatting hiccups (e.g., inline math Ï) reduce polish
Dot-leader alignment in contents could be more uniform
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Band 2: All mandatory presentation elements are present and correctly formatted. Minor stylistic and consistency issues prevent flawless top-band presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The report follows a logical structure (introduction, tools sections, conclusion, bibliography) and includes a table of contents with page references.  Headings are clear and information flows reasonably, satisfying the âappropriate structure throughoutâ descriptor for band 2.

Documents cited (annual reports, Investopedia article, scholarly SWOT paper) are relevant to the acquisition and provide both qualitative and quantitative information.  Depth is acceptable but uneven: ratio figures are lifted without detailed breakdown and some SWOT points lack citation.  The range of ideas is adequate but not extensive, aligning with the ârelevant and sufficiently in-depthâ description at band 3 rather than the richer coverage required for band 4.

All required elements for an SL IA (title page with RQ and key concept, contents page, cited sources, word count) are present.  Minor citation formatting issues do not equate to missing elements, so the top presentation band (2) is awarded.

The appendix and bibliography indicate three formally labelled âsupporting documentsâ (plus Microsoft annual report extracts) which places the submission in the 3â5 document range.  Therefore the band that awards three marks for appropriate quantity is most accurate.

Application is mostly sound but occasionally superficial.  SWOT comments are generic, Ansoff focuses only on market penetration, and ratio commentary explains percentage movements but omits detailed causes or benchmarking.  The relevance to the research question is clear yet not always deeply explored, matching the ârelevance not always clear but adequately appliedâ descriptor (band 3).

The student selects some pertinent data (financial figures before and after the deal, market-share commentary, SWOT points) and incorporates them into tables.  The data set is serviceable but limited; sources for several figures are unclear.  This aligns with âadequate selection and useâ at band 3.

Analysis moves beyond description in places â e.g., linking falling NPM to acquisition costs and discussing cloud-gaming opportunities â but much commentary restates tool outputs without deeper evaluation or integration.  Overall this is âsome analysisâ rather than the largely integrated, mostly effective analysis required for band 4, so band 3 is appropriate.

The research question is directly restated and nominally answered, yet the extent (âto what extentâ) is only qualitatively discussed with no clear overall measure or balanced judgement.  This constitutes a partial rather than explicit, fully developed answer, placing the work in band 2.

Three standard tools (SWOT, Ansoff, Ratio analysis) are selected.  This constitutes an âadequate selectionâ because they cover internal, strategic growth and financial perspectives, but no more advanced or industry-specific frameworks (e.g., Porter, VRIO) are used.  Hence band 3 is the best fit.

The conclusion asserts that Microsoft made a âwise decisionâ and is now more competitive, mirroring evidence from SWOT and ratio sections.  However, it does not rigorously weigh counter-arguments and relies on general statements rather than detailed evidence, so consistency is partial rather than full.  Band 2 therefore fits best.

The student demonstrates a clear grasp of Microsoftâs and Activision Blizzardâs situations, drawing on background, strategy, financial data and competitive context.  Each analytical section refers back to the organisation (e.g., market penetration after the deal, ratio changes, loyalty of Blizzardâs community).  The analysis is coherent but not seamlessly woven through every paragraph; some assertions about reputation or share price are generic and weakly evidenced.  This meets the âstrong analysis with partial integrationâ level rather than a fully integrated, consistently evidenced treatment expected for the top band.

The student keeps the key concept of change at the forefront, repeatedly linking the acquisition to alterations in Microsoftâs market share, brand image, product range and financial structure.  References to âsignificant changeâ, âchanged its positionâ, and âradically changed the landscape of cloud gamingâ appear in every major section, showing that the idea is not an after-thought but a guiding lens.  However, while the concept is present throughout, the use is occasionally superficial â the word âchangeâ is sometimes inserted rather than critically explored.  Because understanding is solid and usually connected to analysis but not fully integrated at a sophisticated level, the work best fits the âstrong understanding with partially integrated analysisâ descriptor (band 4).

annotations.comment: Page numbers in the contents table are aligned but could be formatted uniformly (e.g., dot leaders) for improved readability and presentation consistency.
Consider numbering sub-sections in the contents (e.g., SWOT 2.1) to match in-text headings, ensuring structural consistency throughout.
The introduction contextualizes both companies and mentions change, but it would benefit from defining how âchangeâ is conceptualized in strategic terms.
The list of supporting documents is described but the footnotes use placeholders (e.g., Supporting Document 2) without proper in-text citation formatting.
SWOT footnotes repeatedly reference âSupporting Document 3â, which creates ambiguity; use specific document titles or numbering consistently.
The research question is clear but could be rephrased to focus on measurable outcomes, such as specific metrics for competitiveness, to sharpen the investigation.
The key concept âChangeâ is stated but not defined in context; include a concise theoretical definition and explain its dimensions to strengthen integration.
All required elements (title page, research question, key concept, word count) are present and correctly formatted, satisfying presentation requirements.
The table of contents is clear and logically organized, aiding navigation through the reportâs sections.
The rationale highlights the importance of the question but could tie the concept of change directly to specific data metrics to guide analysis.
The SWOT table includes generic entries (e.g., âCompetitionâ) without specifying key rivals or market metrics, limiting analytical depth.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The Microsoft News Center entry contains inline math (Ï) which is inappropriate in citation; remove or correct this formatting error.
To integrate the key concept, link each SWOT element back to how the acquisition drove change in Microsoftâs strategic competitiveness.
SWOT strengths description is descriptive but lacks critical trade-off analysis; consider evaluating negative implications of each strength.
The weaknesses section on page 5 ends mid-sentence and lacks full elaboration; complete the analysis with clear examples of residual risks.
Explicitly connect each SWOT section back to the research question on competitiveness to reinforce analytical coherence.
The threats discussion remains at a descriptive level; include industry data (e.g., competitor market shares) to deepen evaluation.
The opportunities narrative overlaps earlier points; consider merging repeated ideas to maintain concise structure.
The section header âThreatâ is singular whereas others are plural; ensure consistent terminology (Threats/Opportunities).
The Ansoff Matrix is plotted but only market penetration is analyzed; discuss other quadrants (product development, diversification) for fuller coverage.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The opening sentence about cloud gaming âradically changedâ lacks data or citation; specify metrics or a source to substantiate the claim.
Ansoff model footnote lacks direct in-text citation formatting; ensure consistency with academic referencing style.
The ratio formulas are correctly rendered using LaTeX notation, enhancing clarity and professional presentation.
Profitability ratios are discussed but lack industry benchmarks; include competitor or sector averages to contextualize Microsoftâs performance.
The ratio table is clear but missing direct source citations for each data point; add footnotes or a legend indicating origins.
Liquidity analysis explains minor ratio changes but should link how liquidity supports Microsoftâs strategic flexibility post-acquisition.
The conclusion block begins mid-page rather than as a distinct closing section; restructure to clearly delineate the conclusion from analysis.
Conclusion lacks discussion of counter-arguments or potential risks of the acquisition; include balanced evaluation to strengthen validity.
Final conclusion restates the research question but does not quantify âextentâ; provide explicit measures or comparative statements.
The conclusion prose is engaging but consider summarizing key findings in bullet points to enhance clarity and impact.
Bibliography includes all required sources but formatting is inconsistent (e.g., italics, inline math symbols); apply a uniform citation style.
Extra sources are useful but should be clearly labeled as supplementary rather than core supporting documents.
